coredata.dc:title: Effect of carbon dot-doped Ti-MOF on CMC/Agar film and active packaging application on storage quality of fruits

coredata.dc:description: Ti-metal organic framework (Ti-MOF) doped with carbon dots (CDs) with enhanced antibacterial potential was synthesized using solvothermal-assisted mechanical stirring and used for the fabrication of CMC/Agar-based active packaging films. The incorporation of CD@Ti-MOF not only improved the tensile strength of the CMC/Agar film by 17.4% but also exhibited strong antioxidant activity with 100% of ABTS and 57.8% of DPPH radical scavenging using 0.64 cm2/mL of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film. Furthermore, water vapor permeability, oxygen permeability, and ultraviolet light-blocking ability (95.7% of UV-B and 84.7% of UV-A) were improved significantly. The CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film showed strong antibacterial activity and could inhibit the progress of E. coli up to 8.2 Log CFU/mL and completely stopped the growth of L. monocytogenes after 12 h of incubation. Additionally, CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film extended the shelf life of cherry tomatoes preserved at 4 °C and delayed the quality degradation, maintaining the visual aspects of the packaging.

head.ce:title: Effect of carbon dot-doped Ti-MOF on CMC/Agar film and active packaging application on storage quality of fruits

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ti-metal organic framework (Ti-MOF) doped with carbon dots (CDs) with enhanced antibacterial potential was synthesized using solvothermal-assisted mechanical stirring and used for the fabrication of CMC/Agar-based active packaging films. The incorporation of CD@Ti-MOF not only improved the tensile strength of the CMC/Agar film by 17.4% but also exhibited strong antioxidant activity with 100% of ABTS and 57.8% of DPPH radical scavenging using 0.64 cm2/mL of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film. Furthermore, water vapor permeability, oxygen permeability, and ultraviolet light-blocking ability (95.7% of UV-B and 84.7% of UV-A) were improved significantly. The CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film showed strong antibacterial activity and could inhibit the progress of E. coli up to 8.2 Log CFU/mL and completely stopped the growth of L. monocytogenes after 12 h of incubation. Additionally, CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film extended the shelf life of cherry tomatoes preserved at 4 °C and delayed the quality degradation, maintaining the visual aspects of the packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fresh fruit quality deteriorates after harvest, resulting in 20–30% loss during the post-harvest storage and distribution stages (Rozman, Hashim, Maringgal, Abdan, & Sabarudin, 2023). Among various fruits, Solanum Lycopersicum var. cerasiforme (cherry tomatoes) is popular worldwide due to its nutritional and organoleptic qualities (de Oliveira et al., 2021). However, due to the juicy flesh tissue and thin epidermis, tomatoes are prone to quality degradation due to microbial contamination (especially fungal contamination), metabolism, and oxidation during post-harvest processing, storage, transportation, and retail stages, which affects their market price (Damas-Job et al., 2023). Therefore, it is necessary to find a way to preserve fresh food appropriately and prolong its shelf life.

Currently, various chemical and physical traditional food preservation methods are used to preserve the freshness of fruits. Chemical approaches include embalming or adding chemical coatings to achieve freshness, while physical approaches include radiation, gas filling, and refrigeration (Liao et al., 2023). However, the high risk of cold temperature effects among fruits limits the cold storage of cold-sensitive products (Ding et al., 2015). Additionally, the overuse of chemical fungicides used to prevent mold growth during post-harvest fruit transportation and storage can cause fungal diseases to develop resistance to the fungicides, threatening human health and the environment.

In this context, modified active packaging, which provides antibacterial and antioxidant activity and can control the O2 concentration within the packaging, appears to be the most suitable approach for the preservation of fresh foods (e.g., vegetables and fruits). The shelf life of fruit can be extended by using packaging materials containing antibacterial agents such as TiO2 (Zhang & Rhim, 2022). The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has accepted the use of titanium-based nanoparticles intended for food additives and food exchange materials (Zheng et al., 2024). Further, Ti nanoparticles promote the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl (HO•), hydroperoxyl (HOO•), and superoxide (O2 •−) (Zheng et al., 2024). It is widely accepted that it can effectually inhibit the development of bacteria and fungi through ROS generation by oxidizing the polyunsaturated phospholipid composition of cell membranes (Khan, Ezati, Kim, & Rhim, 2023).

In this study, a novel hybrid nanocomposite based on titanium metal organic framework (Ti-MOF) doped with carbon dots (CD) was suggested to fabricate CMC/Agar-based antibacterial packaging film for cherry tomato preservation. MOFs are multifunctional materials that have crystalline porous properties and are produced by the self-assembly of organic ligands and transition metal ions (Riahi, Khan, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2023). MOFs, as effective active packaging materials, can be tuned to provide consistent pore size, shape, and desired functionality with high surface-to-volume ratios, along with their structural stability, porosity, and hydrophobicity (Sharanyakanth & Radhakrishnan, 2020). Recently, different MOF particles, including UiO-66-NH2 (Xu et al., 2023), Cr-MOF (Khezerlou et al., 2023), ZIF-8 (Riahi, Hong, Rhim, Shin, & Kim, 2023), and cyclodextrin (Jiang et al., 2023) was applied as an active filler of food packaging materials. However, the functional properties of MOFs can be improved through surface functionalization processes to produce active materials suitable for food packaging purposes. In this regard, CDs (size <10 nm) are considered to be biocompatible as active food ingredients in food coatings and films (Khan, Ezati, Kim, & Rhim, 2023). Therefore, CDs are suggested to dope on the Ti-MOF surface (CD@Ti-MOF) to enhance the functional characteristics of MOF particles. The small particle size of CDs and their close heterostructure with MOF allows the CD to connect through surface functional groups or enter the pores of the MOF (Yan et al., 2022). The functional moieties on the CD surface likely possessed targeting binding sites, which helped improve the overall performance of CD@Ti-MOF nanocomposites. CD@Ti-MOF not only preserves the original morphological properties but also has the advantages of MOF and CD. Additionally, the inorganic-organic hybrid nature of MOFs may contribute to their compatibility with polymer matrices.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is an environmentally friendly and readily available cellulose derivative that can be employed to manufacture films for food packaging due to its hydrophilic and biodegradable properties (Liu et al., 2021). CMC has received special attention in the field of sustainable and functional food packaging amidst two controversies: excessive consumption of petrochemical resources and plastic pollution caused by the use of plastic packaging materials. However, the low mechanical properties and high hydrophilicity of CMC have limited its practical applications. Therefore, in order to improve the properties of CMC film, composite films can be manufactured by blending CMC with other polymers. Agar is a good candidate for this grounds because it has a similar chemical arrangement to CMC, with good film-forming ability and water resistance but relatively lower flexibility issues (Tammina, Priyadarshi, & Rhim, 2023). Since the blend film of agar and CMC can complement the strengths and weaknesses of each biopolymer film, the combination of the two polymers has been proposed as a fruit packaging film with good compatibility (Ezati et al., 2023).

Therefore, the evaluation of the influence of the hybridization of CDs with Ti-MOFs and their interaction with the CMC/Agar polymer matrix in conferring synergistic functionality was attempted for the first time. Uniformly dispersed CD@Ti-MOF nanocomposites provide high tensile strength, can absorb ultraviolet light, prevent photosensitive oxidation of fresh foods, and inhibit the growth of foodborne pathogens. With these advantages, packaging films containing CD@Ti-MOF are expected to be useful for extending the post-harvest storage of cherry tomatoes. Therefore, the main purpose of this study is to manufacture CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film, evaluate its characteristics such as mechanical properties, water contact angle (WCA), water vapor permeability (WVP), oxygen permeability (OP), and optical properties and apply it to the packaging of cherry tomatoes to investigate the effect of extending shelf life.

Agar was purchased from Gel Tec Co. Ltd. (Seoul, Korea), and CMC (average MW: 250,000, the degree of substitution: 0.9) was obtained from Junsei Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Titanium(IV) isopropoxide (MW: 284.2, purity: 97%, density: 0.96 g/cm3), dimethylformamide (DMF), terephthalic acid (TPA), methanol, and ethanol were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 23235, Escherichia coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895, Salmonella enterica, Aspergillus flavus, Penicillium chrysogenum, Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313 were supplied by the KCTC (Korean Collection for Type Culture, Seoul, Korea). Fresh cherry tomatoes for packaging testing were bought from a local supermarket (Seoul, Korea).

Ti-MOFs were produced physicochemically by adopting a solvothermal approach according to a previously published method with some modifications (Abdelhameed, Darwesh, & El-Shahat, 2023). Briefly, 2 g (12 mmol) of TPA was completely dissolved in a mixed solvent of methanol (4 mL) and DMF (36 mL), and then 1.136 mL (0.23 mmol) of C12H28O4Ti was added. The obtained reagent mixture was gently stirred for 20 min at room temperature (25 °C), and the homogeneous solution was shifted to a 100 mL Teflon vessel reactor and treated at 150 °C for 18 h in a muffle furnace. (Fig. 1 ). After cooling, the white crystalline powder was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly three times with ethanol and distilled water. Finally, the obtained Ti-MOF powder was dried and activated at 90 °C in a vacuum oven.

On the other hand, the CD was prepared using a hydrothermal approach. For this, 1.0 g of cumin powder was added to 50 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 20 min. The prepared dispersion was added to a 100 mL Teflon-vessel reactor and heated at 190 °C for 8 h (Fig. 1). After cooling, the yellow-brown CD solution was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min, followed by filtration through a Whatman filter (100 μm). The supernatant was then filtered using a Whatman membrane filter (0.22 μm of pore size, 25 mm diameter nylon filter) to collect the CD solution. Then, CD@Ti-MOF was prepared by mechanically stirring the solvothermal-assisted CD and Ti-MOF particles. For this, a mixture of synthesized Ti-MOF and CD in a ratio of 1:2 (w/w) was mixed with 50 mL of distilled water and mildly stirred at 25 °C for 24 h. Finally, the yellow-brown powder of CD@Ti-MOF was collected by centrifugation, filtration, and washing with distilled water and ethanol.

As displayed in Fig. 1, the CMC/Agar blend-based active packaging film loaded with CD@Ti-MOF was fabricated using a solution casting strategy. For this, 2 wt% CMC aqueous solution was made by dissolving 4.0 g of CMC in 200 mL distilled water containing glycerol (1.2 g) and treated at -85 °C for 1 h under stirring. Separately, 4 g of agar and 1.2 g of glycerol were dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water and heated with stirring at 90 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, the CMC solution and the agar solution were mixed in a 1:1 ratio, and the mixed solution was further stirred at 55 °C for 2 h to obtain a CMC/Agar binary blend.

Separately, both the Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF particles were homogeneously dispersed in distilled water using a probe-type ultrasonic processor (VCX750, Sonics and Materials Inc., Newtown, CT, USA) for 20 min. Then, the film solutions of CMC/Agar-based films were constructed by adding functional fillers, 2 wt% (based on CMC/Agar polymer) of each Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF and heating at 55 °C for 1 h with vigorous agitation. The film solutions (200 mL each) were cast uniformly onto a flat 24 cm × 30 cm Teflon-coated glass plate and dried at 25 °C until the solvent was completely evaporated, after which the resulting films were collected. The prepared films were listed as neat CMC/Agar, CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF, and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF, respectively, depending on the Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF added. The films were physicochemically characterized, as detailed in the supporting information.

To confirm the actual application of the fabricated films, 40 g of cherry tomatoes as a test sample were packaged using CMC/Agar, CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF, and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films (8 cm × 8 cm) sealed with polyethylene plastic wrap and stored at 4 °C for 24 days for quality investigation. The group kept in a Petri dish was designated as the control group for comparison. The quality of packaged specimens was assessed on days 0, 6, 12, 18, and 24 for each group. Quality evaluation of packaged fruits was conducted by measuring weight loss, pH, hardness change, and bacterial growth inhibition effect. The gravimetric method was used to determine weight loss, in which body weight was recorded at regular intervals, and the data were expressed using the average of the percent weight loss. The amount of bacteria grown in the test sample was measured using the TVC (total viable colony count) strategy. To investigate the antibacterial effect of the packaging film, the 5 g test samples were homogenized for 3.0 min in 45 mL peptone water using a stomacher (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France). Then, 1 mL of serially diluted homogenous aliquots was used to duplicate 3 M™ Petri film counting plates. After incubation at 37 °C for 48 h, TVC results were expressed as Log CFU/g.

All tests were performed in triplicate using an individually prepared film, and the results were presented as mean SD (standard deviation). A one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was conducted, and Duncan's multiple range tests were used to determine the significance of each mean value (p < 0.05) using the SPSS statistical analysis software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

FTIR (Fourier transform infrared) investigation was performed to assess the surface functional groups of as-prepared MOF, and the results are depicted in Fig. 2a. The FTIR spectra of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF contain the CH stretching of sp2 or sp3-hybridized carbon (2854 to 2926 cm−1), OH bending (1358 cm−1), and CC stretching vibration (1549 cm−1), depicting the conjugation of sp2-hybridized carbon (Abdelhameed et al., 2023). After doping with CD, the FTIR spectrum of CD@Ti-MOF indicates additional bands: O-H/N-H stretching (3308 cm−1), -C=O carbonyl stretching (1740 cm−1), and CN and C-O/C-N stretching corresponding to 1235 and 1050 cm−1, respectively (Khan, Riahi, Kim, & Rhim, 2024; Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024). These additional peaks are due to the abundance of carboxyl, hydroxyl or amine groups on the surface of as-synthesized CD, which further confirms the successful doping of CD on the Ti-MOF surface. In addition, both the IR spectra comprise the stretching vibration around 538–747 cm-1, indicating the O-Ti-O bands; a sharp peak at 626 cm−1 ascribed to the TiO band's vibrational stretching, whereas bands around 1155–1361 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O–C bands (epoxide group) (Haghighi, Shahlaei, Bahrami, & Targhan, 2021).

Fig. 2b shows the XRD (X-ray diffractometer) patterns of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF particles. For both tested particles, typical crystal diffractograms at 6.7°, 9.8°, 11.7°, 16.4°, and 18.9° can be matched to the (101), (200), (211), (222), and (312) crystal planes, respectively which validate the orthorhombic crystal structure of the synthesized MOF (Han, Lee, & Cho, 2019; Han, Yang, Liu, Li, & Shao, 2019). After doping with CDs, an additional broad peak of CD@Ti-MOF in the range of 23.8° depicted the occurrence of CDs in the MOF structure, similar to the previously published reports (Zhao et al., 2020). The XRD patterns thoroughly overlapped for Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF, suggesting that the crystalline structure was sustained during CD loading into Ti-MOF.

The ABTS [2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)] and DPPH ((2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) radical neutralization assays were adopted to govern the antioxidant action of the Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF, and the outcomes are displayed in Fig. 2c. The undoped Ti-MOF exhibits low radical neutralization action up to a concentration of 600 μg/mL in comparison to the CD-doped CD@Ti-MOF. For Ti-MOF, the ABTS free radical neutralization activity was raised to 28.6 ± 1.8%, while the radicals neutralization efficacy for DPPH was reached up to 12.4 ± 1.4% at the concentration of 600 μg/mL. However, for CD@Ti-MOF, DPPH• and ABTS•+ free radical neutralization efficacies increased to 70.8 ± 2.1% and 98.5 ± 1.8%, respectively, as the concentration raised from 50 to 600 μg/mL. Doping of CD increased the availability of electron-rich -OH and -COOH groups on the CD@Ti-MOF surface, and the radical neutralization effect of DPPH• and ABTS•+ radicals enhanced by H• transfer to DPPH-H and ABTS-H (Khan, Riahi, Kim, & Rhim, 2023; Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024). The antioxidant activity against DPPH was found to be low compared to the ABTS, which could be due to the difference in the polarity of solvents, as discussed in our previous report (Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024).

The surface morphology of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF were observed using FE-SEM (field emission scanning electron microscopy) and TEM (transmission electron microscope) analysis, and the results are shown in Fig. 2d,e. FE-SEM micrographs suggested that the appearance of both the Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF seems to be a cuboid shape in the vicinity of MOF's crystal porous structure, where interstitial spaces such as cavities and crevices are densely packed between the synthesized materials (Fig. 2d,e). The cube sample was identical to the square shape, and upon closer inspection, it can be seen that they have opposing rough edges that may indicate etching marks. The morphology of the Ti-MOF was clear, whereas small granules can be observed on the surface of CD@Ti-MOF, which could be due to the deposition of CDs.

TEM images of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF are presented in Fig. 2f, g. The TEM of the obtained Ti-MOF was cuboidal and square-shaped (Fig. 2f). The TEM of CD@Ti-MOF showed circular CD dispersed on the well-defined square cuboid of the modified MOF surface (Fig. 2g). The surface of CD@Ti-MOF is ornamented by CDs, demonstrating that the facets at the two ends are more chemically active due to the increased surface area after loading CDs. The obtained TEM results confirm the successful incorporation of CDs on the surface of synthesized CD@Ti-MOF particles.

Active components based on CD-doped MOFs have received significant interest due to their unique functional ability owing to the combined hybrid characteristics of MOFs, and the CD nucleus. Therefore, CD-doped MOF particles have been recognized as an essential element for the synthesis of heterocyclic compounds with potential pharmacological properties (Li et al., 2021). The bioactive properties of the synthesized CD@Ti-MOF were assessed against four bacterial strains (L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, E. coli, and S. enterica) using the agar well-diffusion method and two fungal strains (P. chrysogenum and A. flavus) by poisoned food technique as shown in Fig. 3a-c. The antibacterial outcomes depicted that pristine Ti-MOF exhibited weak antibacterial activity against all four analyzed bacterial strains, whereas, for CD@Ti-MOF, the bactericidal effect was significantly higher than undoped Ti-MOF (Fig. 3a). Also, inhibition zones of CD@Ti-MOF against L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, S. enterica, and E. coli were 7.5, 10.3, 6.9 and 3.1 mm, respectively. However, the Ti-MOF showed small inhibition zones against S. aureus (1.8 mm) and S. enterica (1.2 mm), while negligible inhibition zones on both L. monocytogenes and E. coli. Moreover, antibacterial results revealed that the bactericidal efficacy of the Ti-MOF was significantly improved after doping with CD (CD@Ti-MOF) against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, as reported in previous findings (Travlou et al., 2018).

On the other hand, CD@Ti-MOF exhibited promising antifungal efficacy against both tested fungal strains (Fig. 3b,c). After 5 days of incubation, it was observed that fungal growth for both P. chrysogenum and A. flavus strains completely covered the surface area in the control group, but a significant inhibition in the fungus growth was observed in the group treated with CD@Ti-MOF. However, the fungal growth inhibition of Ti-MOF was lower compared to CD@Ti-MOF, indicating that adding CD to Ti-MOF enhanced its antifungal activity. This result is consistent with results showing that MOF derivative materials enhance antifungal activity against unicellular and multicellular fungi (Qubais Saeed et al., 2023).

Regarding the mechanism of antimicrobial action, the resulting metal-ligand coordination system's framework, the varied ion concentration balance, and the crystal structure are likely accountable for the antimicrobial efficacy of MOFs (Sharanyakanth & Radhakrishnan, 2020). Due to the linkers and nodes of the MOF framework, organic ligands accord little to antimicrobial action (Sharanyakanth & Radhakrishnan, 2020). The incessant network-like structure has a larger specific surface area, giving bacteria more locations to adhere to the MOF surface and enhancing metal ion exposure (Shen et al., 2020). Furthermore, the abundance of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups present on the CD-doped Ti-MOF surface react with electrons (e−) and photogenerated holes (h+) of transition metal ions and accelerate the generation of ROS such hydroxyl (HO•), hydroperoxyl (HOO•), and superoxide (O2 •−) (Zheng et al., 2024). Referring to the antimicrobial action of CDs, it has been widely investigated that CD binds to the surface of pathogen cells, causing ROS to be produced through the transfer of oxygen molecules (Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024). The generated ROS, such as HO• and O2 •− or 1O2 (singlet molecular oxygen), disrupt the oxidative balance and ultimately destroy biomolecules present in the cell wall, resulting in cell lysis. As a result, the antibacterial activity of CD@Ti-MOF is enhanced because it can effectively inhibit the growth of fungi and bacteria through ROS generation by oxidizing the polyunsaturated phospholipid composition of the cell membrane, in accordance with previous studies. Therefore, it can be concluded that the synthesized CD@Ti-MOF has shown potential in eliminating harmful and perishable microbes and contracting the limits of realizing newly hybrid active food packaging materials.

The FTIR spectra (Fig. 4a) of neat CMC/Agar, CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF, and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films exhibit OH/-NH groups (∼3278 cm−1), stretching modes of CH at 2930 cm−1, CH stretching of sp2 or sp3-hybridized carbon (2876 to 2926 cm−1), CO bending (∼1676 cm−1), and CC stretching vibration of (1588 cm−1) (Abdelhameed et al., 2023; Tammina et al., 2023). The characteristic IR bands around 1030, 1119, and 1421 cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibrations mode CN (due to CD), CO, and -COO- groups, respectively (Khan, Riahi, Kim, & Rhim, 2024). It has been observed that IR spectra for all the tested films were recorded to be with similar absorption bands, which might be due to the similar arrangements of surface functional groups (same elemental composition) in CMC, agar, and CD@Ti-MOF which led to the superimposition or overlapping at 1235 and 1050 cm−1 (CN and C-N/C-O bond respectively), 1676 cm−1 (C=O) and 1588 cm−1 (CH bonds), in the composite films (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023; Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024). Further, the band in CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF at 610 cm−1 ascribed to the TiO bands (Haghighi et al., 2021). After incorporating synthesized MOF into the CMC/Agar films, slight shifting was observed in the characteristic absorption bands for the composite films due to the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between polymer matrices and added MOF (Khan, Riahi, Kim, & Rhim, 2024).

Further, as shown in Fig. 4b, neat CMC/Agar film exhibited a strong and broad peak positioned around 2θ = 20.1° corresponding to the characteristic diffractogram of biopolymers, which indicates the amorphous structure of binary polymer blend (Riahi, Khan, et al., 2023). Furthermore, the additional peaks centered around 2θ = 6.5°, 9.7°, 11.8°, 16.4°, and 19.0° in the diffractograms CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films are similar to the Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF particles (Fig. 2b), confirming the existence of MOF within the active packaging film (Han, Lee, & Cho, 2019; Han, Yang, et al., 2019). In addition, the CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films also displayed the same broad diffraction around 2θ = 20.1°, attributing that the interaction between CMC/Agar, Ti-MOF, and CD@Ti-MOF did not impair the crystal structure of MOF. As a result, the addition of CD@Ti-MOF reinforced the semi-crystalline structure of the fabricated films and further enhanced their physicochemical characteristics.

The chemical composition analysis of fabricated films was assessed from XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) measurement. C1s (285.7 eV) and O1s (531.8 eV) were the major elements of neat CMC/Agar films, whereas S2p (168.2 eV), N1s (400.7 eV), and Ti2p (495.9 eV) were the additional elements confirming the presence of CD@Ti-MOF in the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF composite film as illustrated in Fig. 4c. From the deconvoluted C1s spectra, the carbon components of both the films comprise C-H/C-C (284.5 eV), CC (285.7 eV), CO (287.4 eV), and O=C-O/O=C (288.2 eV) (Riahi, Khan, et al., 2023) (Fig. 4d). However, for the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film, the broadening of the peak at the binding energy of 285.7 eV corresponds to the C=C/C-N bonds. For high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 4e), both films exhibit similar component peaks attributing the bonding structure of C-O-C/-C=O (532.5 eV), OC (531.8 eV), and O=C-O (530.2 eV) (Hou, Li, Sun, Ai, & Wang, 2014; Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). The spectrum of S2p (Fig. 4f) confirms the presence of two characteristic sulfur states of -SO3-C- (167.6 eV) and [SO4]2− (169.1 eV) (Lacey et al., 2016). In the case of N1s deconvoluted spectrum of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film, three relative split peaks ascribed the C-N-C (398.8 eV), NC3 (399.9 eV), and NH (400.1 eV) nitrogen species (Hou et al., 2014) arises due to the CD in the CD@Ti-MOF (Fig. 4g). The peak fitting (Fig. 4h) of Ti2p spectrum unveiled two main peaks at 464.1 eV (Ti 2p 1/2) and 458.4 eV (Ti 2p 3/2), signifying the occurrence of the metal electron transfer sites in the titanium oxo-clusters (Kang et al., 2023).

Surface morphologies and elemental mapping of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF incorporated CMC/Agar films were evaluated by FE-SEM and EDS (energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) (Fig. 5a and Fig. S1, Supporting Information). It can be observed from Fig. 5a that the CMC/Agar film possessed an intact, regular surface with minute wrinkles without loading functional fillers. After incorporating the Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF, the surface of the composite films displayed the assemblies of small granules or clusters, confirming the presence of loaded fillers, as subjected in published reports (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023). However, the cohesiveness, uniformity, and film surface free from wrinkles and cracks remain unaffected even after the loading of CD-modified Ti-MOF (CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film). The main reason behind the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film surface remaining unchanged could be that the surface groups of the CMC/Agar backbone interacted with the -OH and -COOH group of the CD present in the CD@Ti-MOF through hydrogen bonding. Additionally, the cross-section images of CMC/Agar, CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF, and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF were observed to be free from voids and cavities with no cracks or wrinkles. It is supposed that the establishment of electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds provides a continuous structure with no apparent gaps and excellent integrity to the developed films.

Elemental mapping of pure CMC/Agar films suggested that C and O were the major film forming elements, while the presence of Na was due to the sodium salt of CMC (Fig. S1a,b). However, the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF verified the existence of additional N, S, and Ti elements, which confirmed the presence of CD@Ti-MOF in the functional film. The existence of these additional elements with uniform dispersion verified that the added functional filler (CD@Ti-MOF) was compatible with the binary polymer blend (CMC/Agar) (Fig. S1b). In addition, the EDS elemental spectra shown in Fig. S1c depicted the locations and concentrations of C, S, N, O, and Ti elements in the fabricated composite films.

Fig. 5b shows the digital images of the fabricated CMC/Agar, CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF, and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films and the quantitative determination of their color parameters (L, a, b, and ΔE) are listed in Table 1 . The neat CMC/Agar film had a high lightness value (L = 91.1) and was found to be transparent. The addition of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF exhibited negligible impact on the lightness of the CMC/Agar-based composite film (p > 0.05) with slightly changing a-values (Table 1). However, the b-value was significantly increased (p < 0.05) with the addition of CD@Ti-MOF because of the yellow color of the CDs, which produced a faint yellowish tint of the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film. The total color difference (ΔE) of the composite films did not change in a significant manner; therefore, lightness and visually uniform morphology indicate good dispersion of MOF in the CMC/Agar matrix.

Light transmittance measurement was recorded in the range of 200–800 nm to demonstrate the transparency and UV-blocking abilities of the fabricated films (Fig. 5c). The neat CMC/Agar film exhibited high transmittance of light throughout the recorded wavelengths, but the incorporation of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF particles significantly reduced the light transmittance of CMC/Agar-based composite films. The transparency (T660) and UV-blocking (T280) abilities of the neat CMC/Agar film were 88.0% and 42.1%, indicating that the film is transparent to visible and UV light (Table 1). However, It is worthwhile to note that the transparency (T660) of the film was lowered by 33.2 to 40.7%, while the UV-blocking ability (T280) was significantly increased by 91.4 to 97.5% with the addition of the Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF particles, respectively.

The UV blocking performance of CMC/Agar film-based films in the UV-A and UV-B regions is displayed in Fig. 5 c,d. The insignificant UV-blocking ability of the neat CMC/Agar film in the UV-A and B regions was due to the polymer's inherent UV-absorbing properties. It can be seen that the Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF-loaded CMC/Agar have strong UV-blocking abilities in both UV regions compared to the neat polymer film (Fig. 5d). The improved UV-blocking ability of CD@Ti-MOF-added films is due to the fact that the added fillers reflect or absorb the UV radiations and impede the transmission of UV rays (Khan, Riahi, Kim, & Rhim, 2023; Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024). Excellent UV-blocking attributes were demonstrated by the composite film, which makes it an effective packaging material for foods that are sensitive to UV light, such as vitamins, fresh food, and natural colors (Ezati et al., 2023).

The mechanical characteristics of the prepared composite films, which is a crucial parameter for preventing physical damage to packaged products, were assessed in terms of the elastic modulus (EM), tensile strength (TS), and elongation at break (EB), as listed in Table 2 . The neat CMC/Agar film's thickness (58.8 ± 1.2 μm) was increased slightly due to the rise in the solid content as a result of loading Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF particles (Riahi, Khan, et al., 2023). It was noticed that incorporating Ti-MOF can perform as collective materials for the CMC/Agar matrix, which might be related to the activity of Ti-MOF via the electron's lone pair localized on oxygen moieties that acts as a covenant electron to connect the film networks (El-Naggar, Hasanin, & Hashem, 2022). As a result, the TS of CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF film was increased by almost 20.1% (66.4 ± 2.5 MPa) compared to the neat CMC/Agar (55.3 ± 1.2 MPa), where the firmness (EM) and the EB of the composite film were also increased (Table 2). Moreover, the TS of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film slightly decreased due to the filler content effect of CD-doped Ti-MOF. However, the EB and EM increase might be due to the hydrophilic nature of CD, which acts as a plasticizer that improves the biopolymer film molecular mobility within the film matrix, thereby slightly decreasing the TS and increasing the flexibility of composite film (Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023; Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024). The primary function of Ti-MOF is associated with the lone pairs of electrons that facilitate the interaction of CMC/Agar composite packaging films (Zhang & Rhim, 2022). Moreover, incorporating CD@Ti-MOF also plays a significant role in improving the mechanical properties of proposed films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: WCA, WVP and OP of the CMC/Agar films
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF on the surface wettability of CMC/Agar films, the WCA (water contact angle) was assessed as depicted in Fig. 6a. Neat CMC/Agar has a WCA of 64.1° ± 1.6° unveiling the hydrophilic behavior, which is reliable with the published reports (Tammina et al., 2023). The addition of Ti-MOF into the CMC/Agar matrix leads to the distinct increase of WCA in the CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF film (89.6° ± 1.2°), which may be due to the higher hydrophobicity of Ti-MOF compared to CMC/Agar. It has been widely discussed that the addition of hydrophobic metal nanoparticles enhanced the WCA of the biopolymer-based composite films (Ezati et al., 2023). But the incorporation of CD@Ti-MOF decreases the WCA of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film (80.1° ± 1.8°) in comparison to the CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF film (p < 0.05), which was accredited to the occurrence of relatively hydrophilic groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl) in CD. The interactions between the surface group of CDs can lessen the exposure of hydrophobic Ti-MOF and encourage molecular reorientation on the surface of the film, thus increasing WCA.

The WVP of the CMC/Agar-based composite films is illustrated in Fig. 6b. The WVP of neat CMC/Agar film was 11.3 × 10−10 g.m/m2.Pa.s, and it substantially dropped to 9.6 × 10−10 g.m/m2.Pa.s of CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF. The decrease in the WVP of CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF films could be because of the reduction in hydrophilicity after incorporating hydrophobic Ti-MOF, as demonstrated by the WCA results (Riahi, Hong, et al., 2023). However, a slight increase in the WVP values of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF (9.9 × 10−10 g.m/m2.Pa.s) film was observed compared to that of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF. The following factors may be responsible for the rise in WVP of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films: incorporating CD-doped Ti-MOF modifies the free volume at the polymer interface and increases the permeable molecules of polar water vapor by forming a discontinuous phase between polymer matrices (Riahi, Khan, et al., 2023).

Furthermore, the OP of the neat CMC/Agar films decreased significantly (p < 0.05) with the addition of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF (Fig. 6b). This outcome revealed that a dense and regular network of hydrogen bonds associated with enhanced oxygen barrier characteristics evolved with the addition of Ti-MOF and CD@Ti-MOF in the CMC/Agar films which inhibits the diffusion of oxygen through the film by increasing the tortuosity of the diffusion path. However, the CMC/Agar film loaded with CD@Ti-MOF leads to an increase in the OP value of the film. The doping of Ti-MOF with CD increases the hydroxy and carboxyl groups in the CMC/Agar matrix, which increases the interaction of composite film with oxygen. Therefore, the oxygen permeability was likely to be slightly higher in CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF compared to CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antimicrobial abilities of CMC/Agar-based films
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant ability results of the fabricated films determined by adopting the DPPH and ABTS radicals neutralization ability are shown in Fig. 6c. The antioxidant activity of the neat CMC/Agar film was insignificant (2.4% ABTS•+ and 8.3% DPPH• scavenging activity with a 0.64 cm2/mL CMC/Agar film). The negligible radical scavenging action of the film could be due to the polymer adsorption behavior. However, the same-sized composite films (0.64 cm2/mL) exhibited substantially enhanced scavenging activities in terms of neutralizing 16.6% of DPPH• and ∼ 48.9% ABTS•+ for CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF film, while 57.8% of DPPH• and ∼ 100% ABTS•+ for CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films. The antioxidant results of the tested films are in a good manner with the results of MOF particles (Fig. 2c). The enhanced antioxidant activity of the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films is supposed to be because of the presence of abundant functional groups in CDs and intercalation or physical sorption of active molecules (e.g., peroxide compounds) of Ti-MOF (Karami, Jeibar, Sohrabi, Badoei-dalfard, & Sargazi, 2020).

Further, a total viable colony count method was used to investigate the antibacterial potential of the CMC/Agar-based films against E. coli and L. monocytogenes, as illustrated in Fig. 6d, e. It can be observed from Fig. 6d, e that the neat CMC/Agar film did not exhibit any bactericidal effect against both tested bacterial strains, as either CMC or Agar has no antibacterial efficacy (Tammina et al., 2023). In contrast, the film incorporated with CD@Ti-MOF exhibited relatively higher antibacterial activity than the neat CMC/Agar and CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF films. It is worth remarking that the addition of CD@Ti-MOF into the film (CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF) retarded the growth of E. coli by 8.2 Log CFU/mL, whereas it completely eradicated the growth of L. monocytogenes after 12 h of incubation. The antibacterial efficacy of the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films was primarily owing to the strong antibacterial action of the CD@Ti-MOF, as shown in Fig. 3a. The antibacterial efficacy of CD@Ti-MOF might be associated with its capability to interact with main cellular components, including phospholipids, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids, and increase the permeability of cell membrane (Khan, Riahi, Tae Kim, & Rhim, 2024; Riahi, Hong, et al., 2023). The continuous porous network-like structure of CD@Ti-MOF delivers multiple positions for bacteria to bind the released metal ions. Additionally, the abundant oxygenated moiety of CD accelerates ROS generation and endorses the leakage of intracellular substances, resulting in cell lysis (Chen et al., 2022). The antimicrobial activity of the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films was stronger against L. monocytogenes than against E. coli, similar to CD@/Ti-MOF particles (Fig. 3a).

As shown in Fig. 7a, cherry tomatoes in all packaging samples depicted varying changes after 24 days of storage at 4 °C. The findings during the shelf-life investigation demonstrated an increase in fruit degradation in all treatments (Fig. 7b-e). These changes were mainly characterized by the appearance of visible decay and mildew spots. However, compared to control samples, packaging the cherry tomatoes dramatically reduced the endocarp molds and visible decay, which might be attributable to microbial invasion. Furthermore, the visual appearance in the control, polyethylene, and CMC/Agar packings had darkened and indicated serious quality degradation of packages, but the visible decay of fruits with the CD@Ti-MOF incorporated films was negligible. These findings show that CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF-based films are effective at preserving the aesthetic value of packaged fruits.

After harvest and storage, essential indicators to assess the qualitative features of fresh fruits include pH, hardness, weight loss rate, and TVC (Kwon, Chang, & Han, 2017). The pH of cherry tomatoes was raised during storage in all treatments, as seen in Fig. 7b. However, throughout the storage time, CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF packages had little impact on the pH change of preserved cherry tomatoes (p > 0.05). The enzymatic activities and fruit ripening that eventually led to a fall in acid content may have caused the pH to rise during the shelf-life investigation. Fresh cherry tomatoes typically have a pH of around 3.9 to 4.2, although the type of fruit, level of ripeness, storage environment, and microbial contamination can all impact this value (Misra, Keener, Bourke, Mosnier, & Cullen, 2014). The obtained pH results showed that the pH of the packed fruits was maintained when packaged with CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF films.

As shown in Fig. 7c, the firmness of cherry tomatoes was assessed on days 0, 6, 12, 18 and 24. The results demonstrate that throughout the shelf-life investigation, the firmness considerably decreased for all packaging. This analysis verified that the firmness value of the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film packaging was higher than the control (p > 0.05). Depolymerization, or pectin hydrolyzation, and cellular breakdown caused the fruit softening (Kwon et al., 2017; Paniagua, East, Hindmarsh, & Heyes, 2013).

As shown in Fig. 7d, the rate of weight loss for cherry tomatoes increased steadily during storage. At the completion of the storage period, it was found that the weight loss rate of the control packing was 35.2%, whereas for the CMC/Agar/Ti-MOF and CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF packages were 15.6% and 9.3%, respectively. The weight loss investigation verified the efficacy of the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF towards maintaining the original quality of packaged fruits during storage. The antioxidant potential of CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film may reduce the oxidation of photosensitive organic components to fruit cells and preserve the fruit's actual quality.

Additionally, the initial TVC in the packaged fruits was ∼1.1 Log CFU/g, but it rapidly rose during storage, particularly in the control, polyethylene and CMC/Agar packages the TVC reached ∼5.0, ∼5.3, and ∼ 4.7 Log CFU/g, respectively after 24 days (Fig. 7e). However, the growth of bacteria was noticeably delayed in the packages treated with the CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film (∼2.5 Log CFU/g). The CMC/Agar/CD@Ti-MOF film's antibacterial properties may reduce the invasion of microorganisms on cherry tomatoes and inhibit the rapid increase in respiration rate caused by adversity stress, thereby slowing down weight loss and maintaining firmness and pH. Overall, the results verified that the CD@Ti-MOF integrated CMC/Agar films can be efficiently employed for active packaging to extend the shelf life of post-harvest fruits.

CMC/Agar-based functional films integrated with CD-doped Ti-MOF particles (CD@Ti-MOF) were prepared for active packaging applications. The CD@Ti-MOF showed strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activity, and dispersed in the polymer matrix of CMC/Agar to form a compatible packaging film. The addition of CD@Ti-MOF improved the mechanical, gas barrier, and UV-barrier properties of the CMC/Agar film. Additionally, the composite films showed strong antioxidant activity as well as strong antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria responsible for post-harvest quality degradation of fruits. The use of composite films extends shelf life and maintains the quality of packaged tomatoes stored for 24 days at 4 °C. The CD@Ti-MOF integrated packaging films have a high potential for use in functional, active food packaging applications.
